content:
Hey, wondering if there's any interest in adding a doc about pinning into Documentation/rust. Kernel rust APIs make decent use of it and it wasn't until now that I had to go and try to understand it. Took a while to figure it out too.

Would've been helpful for me to have a short doc on it cuz up until now I haven't had any need for it in my userland projects.

IMO, if there's something specific to the kernel context that'd be helpful to write about pinning it could makes sense in our docs. But if it's general improvements to pinning docs, it probably makes more sense to push them upstream to rust or the nomicon or something like that.

I think it'd be worth a short mention. The standard pin docs are all related to async and self-refs in rust itself. Kernel does a lot of tricky things with pointers which is (rightfully) not mentioned. And it's a bit of a strange concept to pin for someone coming from C
Is there somekind of task board that me as a noob can look at?
We do not have a detailed task list. We are working on putting together a list of ongoing work though.

Andreas Hindborg said:

We do not have a detailed task list. We are working on putting together a list of ongoing work though.

Andreas, is there any task for a noob or maybe some mentored task?

If you can think of a good example that uses Pin/PhantomPinned in the way kernel does, I don’t think they’d object to a PR on rust-lang/rust to add it. I’ve added docs for a few things that I thought were a bit lacking with no reaistance

Jhonny Vargas said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

We do not have a detailed task list. We are working on putting together a list of ongoing work though.

Andreas, is there any task for a noob or maybe some mentored task?

As far as I know there is currently no list like that, sorry. In general, only patches that are required by some user (a driver) are accepted into the kernel. You could look into implementing a driver for some peripheral you use and then the dependencies of that driver will give you a task list.

I guess you could check out the issue list on github, but atcjes for the issues will only be accepted into the kernel if there is a user. Also, as far as I understand, our github no longer accepts pull requests. Patches should go to the relevant kernel mailing list. That is rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org for core Rust things. @Miguel Ojeda please correct me if I'm wrong.

Andreas Hindborg said:

As far as I know there is currently no list like that, sorry. In general, only patches that are required by some user (a driver) are accepted into the kernel. You could look into implementing a driver for some peripheral you use and then the dependencies of that driver will give you a task list.

There is a "good first issues" tag in GitHub (https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/contribute), but most of those are done or already taken. I added a trivial one the other day, though, which may be helpful for someone learning the whole patch-based workflow, kernel docs, and so on.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I guess you could check out the issue list on github, but atcjes for the issues will only be accepted into the kernel if there is a user. Also, as far as I understand, our github no longer accepts pull requests. Patches should go to the relevant kernel mailing list. That is rust-for-linux@vger.kernel.org for core Rust things. Miguel Ojeda please correct me if I'm wrong.

Thanks Andreas, that sounds about right to me. One note: while we don't merge new PRs there (modulo special cases), sending PRs can still be useful for others to take a look and know what others are working on. For instance, what @Matt Gilbride just sent the other day.
